narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.60, LSAT score of 28, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 93% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure risk led directly to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their standards. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking—all of which worked against you by signaling lower bar passage odds compared to stronger performers. Specifically, your Year 3 decile of 2 (well below the typical 5.5 average) had the strongest negative pull, as students in higher deciles consistently show better bar outcomes. Your LSAT of 28 (far under the average 37) was the next major drag, since higher scores strongly correlate with passing the bar in the model's view of past students. Rounding out the top issues, your Year 1 decile of 2 (versus an average around 6) further reinforced concerns about early academic struggles that often persist. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.60 exceeds the typical 3.21 average and nudged things slightly in a positive direction, it wasn't enough to offset these top concerns. Full-time status is common and neutral here, aligning with most students, and your middle income bracket didn't sway the decision much either. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have LSATs in the mid-30s and deciles above 5, your metrics placed you in a riskier group based on historical bar failure patterns. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many from similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw clear red flags in your rankings and test performance. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your upward trajectory. Schools value persistence, and addressing these key areas could flip the prediction in your favor.
